plainMeaning: This is the confession for the ninth vow, Sāmāyika — sitting in calm, attachment-and-aversion-free equanimity for about 48 minutes — and it lists the small ways a person spoils that calm with mind, speech or body, or by doing it carelessly or incompletely.
recitation: e traṇa guṇavrata huā, have cāra śikṣāvrata kahe che. navamuṁ sāmāyika vrata. samo rāgadveṣa rahita bhāva kīje, te sāmāyika kahīe. e navamā sāmāyika vratataṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ. maṇaduppaṇihāṇe, vayaduppaṇihāṇe, kāyaduppaṇihāṇe, sāmāiyassa akaraṇayā, sāmāiyassa aṇavuṭṭhiyassa karaṇayā, sāmāyika kīdhe ārttadhyāna, raudradhyāna, āhaṭṭa, dohaṭṭa cintavī manaduḥpraṇidhāna kīdhuṁ hoya. vacane karī jā, āva, le, de, āṇa, mūka isyuṁ sāvadya vacana bolyuṁ hoya. ughāḍe mukhe vāta kīdhī hoya. kāyāe karī paḍilehyā pramārjyā pākhe khamāsamaṇa, vāndaṇāṁ dīdhāṁ hoya. hātha, paga, āsana halāvyā hoya. chatī velāe sāmāyika kīdhuṁ na hoya. karīne vismṛta kīdhuṁ hoya. anavasthita-sāmāyika karatāṁ velā avelā joi na hoya. be ghaḍī pūgī pākhe pāryuṁ hoya. pārīne arahuṁ parahuṁ paribhramaṇa kīdhuṁ hoya. kaṇa, kapāśīyā, phūla, phala, māṭī, pāṇī, strī ādikataṇo saṅghaṭṭa (strīo kahe puruṣādikataṇo saṅghaṭṭa) huo hoya. nidrā, vikathādika pramāda kīdhāṁ hoya. sacittane saṅghaṭṭe iriyāvahī paḍikkamī na hoya. aneruṁ e navamā sāmāyikavrata viṣe.

wordByWord.gloss: those three guṇavratas are (now) done/completed
wordByWord.etymology: e (this/those) + traṇa 'three' (Skt. trīṇi) + guṇavrata + huā 'became/were' (from Skt. bhū)
wordByWord.notes: A header line, not part of the numbered confession; it closes vows 6–8.

wordByWord.gloss: now
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati have, from Apabhraṁśa havai (Skt. adhunā/atra)

wordByWord.gloss: the four śikṣāvratas are (now) declared
wordByWord.etymology: cāra 'four' (Skt. catur) + śikṣāvrata + kahe che 'is said' (Skt. kathayati)
wordByWord.notes: Announces the disciplinary-vow group (vows 9–12); Sāmāyika is the first of them.

wordByWord.gloss: ninth
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati numeral nine

wordByWord.gloss: the ninth
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. navama 'ninth' + Gujarati neuter -uṁ

wordByWord.gloss: the Sāmāyika vow (vow of equanimity)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sāmāyika, from sama 'equal/even' + aya/āya; 'the state of evenness' + vrata 'vow'

wordByWord.gloss: to cultivate an even disposition free of attachment and aversion
wordByWord.etymology: samo 'even/equal' (Skt. sama) + rāga 'attachment' + dveṣa 'aversion' + rahita 'devoid of' + bhāva 'inner state' + kīje 'is to be made' (Skt. kṛ)
wordByWord.notes: The text's own working definition of Sāmāyika.

wordByWord.gloss: that, we call Sāmāyika
wordByWord.etymology: te 'that' + sāmāyika + kahīe 'is called' (Skt. kathyate)

wordByWord.token: એ નવમા સામાયિક વ્રતતણા પાંચ અતિયાર શોધું
wordByWord.translit: e navamā sāmāyika vratataṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ
wordByWord.gloss: I now scrutinise the five atichāras of this ninth, Sāmāyika, vow
wordByWord.etymology: vrata + -taṇā (Gujarati genitive 'of') + pāñca 'five' (Skt. pañca) + atiyāra (= atichāra) + śodhuṁ 'I purify/examine' (Skt. śodhayāmi)
wordByWord.notes: The recurring purification formula that opens each block (explained in the preface).

wordByWord.gloss: mis-application of the mind (mano-duṣpraṇidhāna)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit maṇa (Skt. manas 'mind') + duppaṇihāṇa (Skt. duṣpraṇidhāna 'wrong/ill direction'); locative -e
wordByWord.notes: First of the five canonical atichāra-names, in Ardhamāgadhī.

wordByWord.gloss: mis-application of speech (vāg-duṣpraṇidhāna)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vaya (Skt. vāc/vacana 'speech') + duppaṇihāṇa (Skt. duṣpraṇidhāna)

wordByWord.gloss: mis-application of the body (kāya-duṣpraṇidhāna)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kāya (Skt. kāya 'body') + duppaṇihāṇa (Skt. duṣpraṇidhāna)

wordByWord.gloss: the non-performance of the sāmāyika
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sāmāiya + genitive -ssa (Skt. sāmāyikasya 'of the sāmāyika') + a- 'not' + karaṇayā 'doing' (Skt. akaraṇatā)

wordByWord.gloss: performing the sāmāyika unsteadily / without being properly established
wordByWord.etymology: sāmāiyassa (gen.) + aṇavuṭṭhiya (Skt. anavasthita 'not settled/unsteady') + karaṇayā 'doing' (Skt. karaṇatā)
wordByWord.notes: Fifth atichāra; the Prakrit list of the five matches the Śrāvaka-pratikramaṇa (Vandittu) tradition.

wordByWord.gloss: while/having done the sāmāyika
wordByWord.etymology: sāmāyika + kīdhe 'on doing' (Skt. kṛta)
wordByWord.notes: Begins the vivid Gujarati expansion of mano-duṣpraṇidhāna.

wordByWord.gloss: anguished brooding (ārta-dhyāna)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ārta 'distressed' + dhyāna 'meditation/concentration'
wordByWord.notes: One of the two inauspicious meditations a reciter must shun even mid-Sāmāyika.

wordByWord.gloss: cruel/violent brooding (raudra-dhyāna)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. raudra 'fierce' + dhyāna

wordByWord.gloss: restless, back-and-forth brooding (turning a matter over this way and that)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit reduplicative idiom āhaṭṭa-dohaṭṭa, conveying agitated 'one-way-and-the-other' mental churning
wordByWord.notes: Idiomatic Prakrit pair; rendered here from context as anxious wavering thought — see notes for the variant readings.

wordByWord.gloss: having thought/dwelt upon
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. cintayati 'thinks'; gerund cintavī

wordByWord.gloss: one may have committed mis-application of the mind
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. manas + duṣpraṇidhāna + kīdhuṁ 'did' (Skt. kṛta) + hoya 'may be' (Skt. bhavati)
wordByWord.notes: The Sanskritised gloss of the first Prakrit atichāra; 'kīdhuṁ/kīdhāṁ hoya' ('one may have done') is the recurring confessional verb-formula throughout this block.

wordByWord.gloss: by means of speech
wordByWord.etymology: vacane (loc. of Skt. vacana 'speech') + karī 'having done/by' (instrumental sense)
wordByWord.notes: Opens the expansion of vacana-duṣpraṇidhāna.

wordByWord.token: જા, આવ, લે, દે, આણ, મૂક
wordByWord.translit: jā, āva, le, de, āṇa, mūka
wordByWord.gloss: 'go, come, take, give, fetch, put down'
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati imperatives: jā (Skt. yā), āva (ā-gam), le (labh), de (dā), āṇa (ā-nī), mūka (muc)
wordByWord.notes: Sample commanding/worldly utterances — sin-bearing speech to be avoided during Sāmāyika.

wordByWord.gloss: one may have uttered such sin-laden speech
wordByWord.etymology: isyuṁ 'such' + sāvadya 'with fault/sinful' (Skt. sa-avadya) + vacana 'speech' + bolyuṁ 'spoke' + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: one may have talked with the mouth uncovered
wordByWord.etymology: ughāḍe 'open/uncovered' + mukhe 'at the mouth' (Skt. mukha) + vāta 'talk' (Skt. vārtā) + kīdhī hoya 'may have done'
wordByWord.notes: Speaking without covering the mouth (mukhavastrikā/muhpatti) risks harming air-bodied beings.

wordByWord.gloss: by means of the body
wordByWord.etymology: kāyā (Skt. kāya 'body') + karī 'by/having done'
wordByWord.notes: Opens the expansion of kāya-duṣpraṇidhāna.

wordByWord.gloss: without (first) inspecting and sweeping (the spot)
wordByWord.etymology: paḍilehyā 'inspected' (Skt. pratilekhita) + pramārjyā 'swept clean' (Skt. pramārjita) + pākhe 'without' (Gujarati)
wordByWord.notes: Pratilekhana–pramārjana, the careful checking and brushing of a spot before use, to protect tiny beings.

wordByWord.gloss: one may have offered the khamāsamaṇa obeisance and the vāndaṇā veneration
wordByWord.etymology: khamāsamaṇa (Prakrit kṣamāśramaṇa 'forgiving ascetic', the bow) + vāndaṇāṁ (Skt. vandanaka) + dīdhāṁ 'gave/offered' (Skt. datta) + hoya
wordByWord.notes: Fault here = performing these prostrations without the prior inspection/sweeping.

wordByWord.gloss: one may have moved the hands, feet or seat
wordByWord.etymology: hātha 'hand' (Skt. hasta) + paga 'foot' (Skt. pāda) + āsana 'seat' + halāvyā 'moved/shook' + hoya

wordByWord.token: છતી વેલાએ સામાયિક કીધું ન હોય
wordByWord.translit: chatī velāe sāmāyika kīdhuṁ na hoya
wordByWord.gloss: one may have failed to do the sāmāyika though time was available
wordByWord.etymology: chatī 'available/present' (Skt. san/sat) + velāe 'at the time' (Skt. velā) + na 'not'
wordByWord.notes: Expands the fourth atichāra (akaraṇatā), non-performance.

wordByWord.gloss: or, having begun it, may have forgotten it
wordByWord.etymology: karīne 'having done' + vismṛta 'forgotten' (Skt. vi-smṛ) + kīdhuṁ hoya
wordByWord.notes: Forgetfulness (smṛti-lapse), the inner side of the fourth atichāra.

wordByWord.gloss: while doing the sāmāyika unsteadily
wordByWord.etymology: anavasthita 'unsettled' (Skt.) + sāmāyika + karatāṁ 'while doing'
wordByWord.notes: Expands the fifth atichāra (aṇavuṭṭhiyassa karaṇayā).

wordByWord.gloss: one may not have observed the proper and improper times
wordByWord.etymology: velā '(right) time' + avelā 'wrong time' (a- + velā) + joi 'having seen/observed' + na hoya

wordByWord.token: બે ઘડી પૂગી પાખે પાર્યું હોય
wordByWord.translit: be ghaḍī pūgī pākhe pāryuṁ hoya
wordByWord.gloss: one may have concluded it before the two ghaḍī (≈48 minutes) were completed
wordByWord.etymology: be 'two' (Skt. dvi) + ghaḍī 'a 24-minute unit' + pūgī 'reached/completed' + pākhe 'without' + pāryuṁ 'ended/concluded' (Skt. pāra) + hoya
wordByWord.notes: The prescribed Sāmāyika span is two ghaḍī = roughly 48 minutes.

wordByWord.token: પારીને અરહું પરહું પરિભ્રમણ કીધું હોય
wordByWord.translit: pārīne arahuṁ parahuṁ paribhramaṇa kīdhuṁ hoya
wordByWord.gloss: after ending it, one may have wandered about here and there
wordByWord.etymology: pārīne 'having ended' + arahuṁ parahuṁ 'hither and thither' (Gujarati idiom) + paribhramaṇa 'roaming' (Skt. pari-bhram) + kīdhuṁ hoya

wordByWord.token: કણ, કપાશીયા, ફૂલ, ફલ, માટી, પાણી, સ્ત્રી આદિકતણો સંઘટ્ટ
wordByWord.translit: kaṇa, kapāśīyā, phūla, phala, māṭī, pāṇī, strī ādikataṇo saṅghaṭṭa
wordByWord.gloss: contact with grain, cottonseed, flower, fruit, earth, water, a woman, and the like
wordByWord.etymology: kaṇa 'grain' + kapāśīyā 'cottonseed' + phūla 'flower' (Skt. puṣpa) + phala 'fruit' + māṭī 'earth/clay' (Skt. mṛttikā) + pāṇī 'water' (Skt. pānīya) + strī 'woman' + ādika 'et cetera' + -taṇo (gen.) + saṅghaṭṭa 'touch/contact' (Skt. saṅghaṭṭa)
wordByWord.notes: Several listed items are sacitta (living/animate); contact disturbs the vow and harms beings.

wordByWord.gloss: (women say: contact with a man and the like)
wordByWord.etymology: strīo 'women' + kahe 'say' + puruṣa 'man' + ādika 'etc.' + -taṇo (gen.) + saṅghaṭṭa
wordByWord.notes: An in-text rubric adapting the confession for a female reciter.

wordByWord.gloss: may have occurred
wordByWord.etymology: huo 'happened' (Skt. bhū) + hoya 'may be'

wordByWord.gloss: one may have indulged in heedlessness such as sleep and idle gossip
wordByWord.etymology: nidrā 'sleep' + vikathā 'idle/harmful talk' (Skt.) + ādika 'etc.' + pramāda 'negligence/heedlessness' (Skt.) + kīdhāṁ hoya
wordByWord.notes: Sleep and vikathā are classic forms of pramāda that ruin equanimity.

wordByWord.token: સચિત્તને સંઘટ્ટે ઇરિયાવહી પડિક્કમી ન હોય
wordByWord.translit: sacittane saṅghaṭṭe iriyāvahī paḍikkamī na hoya
wordByWord.gloss: on touching an animate thing, one may not have performed the iriyāvahī confession
wordByWord.etymology: sacitta 'with consciousness/living' (Skt. sa-citta) + saṅghaṭṭe 'on contact' + iriyāvahī (Skt. īryāpathikī, the movement-faults confession) + paḍikkamī 'confessed' (Skt. pratikrānta) + na hoya
wordByWord.notes: Touching a living being obliges the Īryāvahiyaṁ confession; omitting it is a transgression.

wordByWord.gloss: and whatever else (was done) regarding this ninth, Sāmāyika, vow
wordByWord.etymology: aneruṁ 'other/anything else' (Skt. anya) + e 'this' + navamā 'ninth' + sāmāyikavrata + viṣe 'concerning' (Skt. viṣaye)
wordByWord.notes: The standard catch-all close; the implied refrain 'te savi micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' follows (per the preface).

literalTranslation: Those three guṇavratas are done; now the four śikṣāvratas are declared. Ninth, the Sāmāyika vow: to make an even disposition free of attachment and aversion — that we call Sāmāyika. I scrutinise the five atichāras of this ninth, Sāmāyika, vow: mis-application of mind, of speech, of body; non-performance of the sāmāyika; and performing the sāmāyika unsteadily. While doing the sāmāyika, having brooded in anguished and cruel meditation, in this-way-and-that restless thought, one may have committed mis-application of the mind. By speech, one may have uttered such sin-laden words as 'go, come, take, give, fetch, put down'; one may have talked with the mouth uncovered. With the body, one may have offered the khamāsamaṇa and vāndaṇā without first inspecting and sweeping the spot; one may have moved hands, feet or seat. Though time was available, one may not have done the sāmāyika, or having begun it may have forgotten it. While doing it unsteadily, one may not have watched the proper and improper times; one may have ended it before the two ghaḍī were complete; after ending, one may have wandered here and there. Contact may have occurred with grain, cottonseed, flower, fruit, earth, water, a woman and the like (for women: with a man and the like). One may have indulged in heedlessness such as sleep and gossip. On touching a living thing one may not have made the iriyāvahī confession — and whatever else regarding this ninth Sāmāyika vow.
idiomaticTranslation: The three multiplying vows are complete; now the four disciplinary vows begin. The ninth is Sāmāyika. To settle into an even, balanced heart, untouched by attachment or aversion — that is what we call Sāmāyika. Let me now examine its five faults: wrong direction of mind, of speech, of body; failing to keep the vow at all; and keeping it unsteadily. Perhaps, even while seated in Sāmāyika, my mind slipped into anxious or cruel brooding, turning some worry over and over — that is mind misused. Perhaps my tongue let slip worldly commands — 'go, come, take, give, fetch, set down' — sinful speech; perhaps I spoke with my mouth uncovered. Perhaps my body offered its bows and venerations without first inspecting and gently sweeping the spot; perhaps I fidgeted my hands, feet, or seat. Perhaps I let the hour pass and never sat at all, or sat and then forgot. Perhaps, keeping it carelessly, I ignored the right and wrong moments, rose before the full forty-eight minutes were done, and then drifted about aimlessly. Perhaps I brushed against grain, cottonseed, flower, fruit, earth, water, or a person — many of them living beings. Perhaps I sank into sleep and idle chatter. Perhaps, having touched a living thing, I neglected the iriyāvahī confession. For these, and whatever else I did against this ninth vow — may that misdeed of mine come to nothing.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse opens the śikṣāvrata quartet and turns inward in a way the preceding economic vows (karmādāna, anartha-daṇḍa) did not: where those policed outward livelihood, Sāmāyika polices the quality of a single 48-minute interval of stillness. The text first gives its own crisp definition — samo rāgadveṣa rahita bhāva, 'an even state void of attachment and aversion' — then audits that interval with the fixed five-atichāra grid. Notice the architecture: the three duṣpraṇidhāna faults (mind, speech, body) cover how one sits; akaraṇatā covers not sitting; and anavasthita-karaṇatā covers sitting badly — together an exhaustive sweep of presence and quality. The Gujarati expansion is strikingly concrete. Mental fault is illustrated by ārta- and raudra-dhyāna and the vivid Prakrit idiom āhaṭṭa-dohaṭṭa (churning a worry to and fro); speech-fault by six bare imperatives — jā, āva, le, de, āṇa, mūka — the very reflexes of household command that a vow of equanimity must silence, plus talking with an uncovered mouth (endangering air-bodied life). Bodily fault is subtler: even performing the sacred khamāsamaṇa and vāndaṇā becomes a transgression if done without pratilekhana–pramārjana, the prior inspecting and sweeping. The fourth and fifth atichāras are then dramatised in everyday failure — letting the hour slip, forgetting mid-rite, misjudging the time, rising before the two ghaḍī close, then wandering off, brushing against sacitta things, dozing, gossiping, and omitting the obligatory iriyāvahī after contact with a living being. The gendered rubric (strīo kahe puruṣādika…) shows the confession was meant to be voiced verbatim by both men and women. The closing aneruṁ… formula gathers every unnamed slip into the same release.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Sāmāyika is the layperson's nearest approach to the monk's state: for two ghaḍī the śrāvaka adopts sarva-sāvadya-yoga-viramaṇa, the suspension of all sinful activity of mind, speech and body — which is exactly why the three duṣpraṇidhāna head the atichāra list, mapping onto the three yogas through which karma is bound. The demand to avoid ārta- and raudra-dhyāna situates Sāmāyika within the fourfold dhyāna scheme: the rite is meant to be a foothold toward dharma- and śukla-dhyāna, so lapsing into the two inauspicious meditations defeats its purpose from within. The insistence on pratilekhana, on the iriyāvahī after sacitta-contact, and on shunning sleep and vikathā ties equanimity directly to ahiṁsā and apramāda — inner calm is inseparable from outward carefulness toward jīvas. Note that this list's last two members (akaraṇatā, anavasthita-karaṇatā) differ from the Tattvārtha-sūtra's anādara and smṛtyanupasthāna; the verse follows the Āvaśyaka/Śrāvaka-pratikramaṇa recension.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A modern reciter can read this as a precise quality-checklist for any seated practice: did I actually sit (akaraṇatā), sit for the full measure (anavasthita), and keep mind, tongue and body still while sitting? The six imperatives are a pointed mirror for the reflex to manage household and phone mid-practice; 'uncovered mouth' and the sacitta-contact clause translate into simple mindfulness about how and where one sits. The verse trains honesty over perfection — naming the near-misses (started then forgot; ended early; dozed off) rather than only gross failure.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.33 — sāmāyikasya pañca atichārāḥ (kāya-vāṅ-mano-duṣpraṇidhāna, anādara, smṛtyanupasthāna)
Vandittu (Śrāvaka-pratikramaṇa) sūtra — the five sāmāyika atichāras: maṇaduppaṇihāṇe… aṇavuṭṭhiyassa karaṇayā
Īryāpathikī (Iriyāvahiyaṁ) confession for harm while moving / on contact with jīvas
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.)

sources.title: JainQQ — Jain eLibrary (Śrāvaka pratikramaṇa / atichāra texts and commentary)
sources.consultedFor: The canonical five sāmāyika atichāras and the Vandittu-tradition wording maṇaduppaṇihāṇe…aṇavuṭṭhiyassa karaṇayā.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit glosses for duṣpraṇidhāna, anavasthita, ārta/raudra-dhyāna, sāmāyika, sāvadya, sacitta.

sources.title: Tattvārtha-sūtra (Umāsvāti), ch. 7 — vrata and atichāra scheme
sources.consultedFor: Comparison of the verse's atichāra list with the Tattvārtha enumeration (anādara, smṛtyanupasthāna) and the three-yoga/duṣpraṇidhāna doctrine.

notes: The Prakrit pair 'āhaṭṭa dohaṭṭa' is an idiomatic reduplication; the book offers no gloss. It is read here, with most commentarial tradition, as restless 'this-way-and-that' brooding aligned with ārta/raudra-dhyāna; this rendering is marked tentative and other glosses (e.g. dwelling on profit/loss) circulate. The book's spelling 'atiyāra' (ય) and Prakrit forms (sāmāiyassa, aṇavuṭṭhiyassa, paḍikkamī) are preserved verbatim, not normalised. The header line 'e traṇa guṇavrata huā…' is editorial framing within the text, not a numbered atichāra. The verse's last two atichāra-names (akaraṇatā, anavasthita-karaṇatā) follow the Āvaśyaka/Vandittu recension and differ from Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.33 (anādara, smṛtyanupasthāna) — a genuine recensional variant, not an error. The closing 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' is implied by the recurring formula described in the preface and not reprinted in this verse's excerpt. The archive.org URL points to the relevant edition repository rather than an independently re-verified page.
